verses.text: It is spring, 'the time when kings go forth to battle' — but David sends Joab and stays home in Jerusalem. The king is where he ought not to be. Idleness and ease, away from the place of duty, open the door through which the worst temptations walk in.

From his roof David sees a beautiful woman bathing, sends to ask, learns she is married — and takes her anyway. One unguarded glance becomes inquiry, then desire, then adultery. Sin grows step by step when the heart, instead of fleeing, lingers and reaches out.

From his roof David sees a beautiful woman bathing, sends to ask, learns she is married — and takes her anyway. One unguarded glance becomes inquiry, then desire, then adultery. Sin grows step by step when the heart, instead of fleeing, lingers and reaches out.

Bathsheba is pregnant, and David schemes to hide it: he recalls her husband Uriah from the front, urging him home. The first sin now breeds a second — concealment. Once we will not confess, we are driven to cover one wrong with another.

But loyal Uriah will not go home to comfort while the army camps in the field; even made drunk, he sleeps at the palace door. The integrity of a soldier shames the king's plotting. Sometimes the faithfulness of an ordinary man exposes the corruption of the great.

His cover-up failed, David writes Uriah's death warrant and sends it by Uriah's own hand: set him in the front, then withdraw. Joab obeys, and Uriah falls. Adultery has now bred murder. Unconfessed sin, cornered, will kill to protect itself.

Joab sends word of the battle, coaching the messenger to soften the king's anger by mentioning Uriah's death last. The crime has made the commander a co-conspirator. Sin spreads its stain, drawing others into its secrets and its guilt.

Hearing Uriah is dead, David sends back cold comfort: 'the sword devoureth one as well as another.' He shrugs off the murder as the fortune of war. A heart hardening in sin grows callous, calling its worst deeds the ordinary cost of doing business.

Bathsheba mourns, then David takes her as wife, and the matter seems closed and quiet. But the chapter ends with the only verdict that counts: 'the thing that David had done displeased the LORD.' Men may see a tidy ending; God sees the sin, and He is not silent.
